section.title: The Holiness of Offerings
section.sectionRationale: This continues the priestly holiness laws of chapter 21, now focused on how priests must handle the sacred offerings, who within priestly households may eat them, and the standard every sacrificial animal must meet, all guarding God's holy name from being profaned.
section.thesis: God requires that priests approach the sacred offerings only in a state of cleanness, that only the priestly household may eat them, and that every animal offered to Him be without blemish, because His holy name must not be profaned by careless handling of what is holy.
section.frameNote: Three movements: priests must be clean to eat the sacred offerings, and only priestly household members may eat them; every offering brought to the LORD, whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering, must be without blemish or defect; and the closing call not to profane God's holy name, since He is the LORD who sanctifies and redeemed Israel from Egypt.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell Aaron and his sons to treat with respect the {{qodashim|sacred offerings}} that the Israelites have consecrated to Me, so that they do not profane My holy name. I am the LORD.

Tell them that for the generations to come, if any of their descendants in a state of uncleanness approaches the sacred offerings that the Israelites consecrate to the LORD, that person must be cut off from My presence. I am the LORD.

If a descendant of Aaron has a skin disease or a discharge, he may not eat the sacred offerings until he is clean. Whoever touches anything defiled by a corpse or by a man who has an emission of semen,
or whoever touches a crawling creature or a person that makes him unclean, whatever the uncleanness may be,
the man who touches any of these will remain unclean until evening. He must not eat from the sacred offerings unless he has bathed himself with water.
When the sun has set, he will become clean, and then he may eat from the sacred offerings, for they are his food.
He must not eat anything found dead or torn by wild animals, which would make him unclean. I am the LORD.
The priests must keep My charge, lest they bear the guilt and die because they {{chalal|profane}} it. I am the LORD who sanctifies them.
No one outside a priest’s family may eat the sacred offering, nor may the guest of a priest or his hired hand eat it.
But if a priest buys a slave with his own money, or if a slave is born in his household, that slave may eat his food.
If the priest’s daughter is married to a man other than a priest, she is not to eat of the sacred contributions.
But if a priest’s daughter with no children becomes widowed or divorced and returns to her father’s house, she may share her father’s food as in her youth. But no outsider may share it.
If anyone eats a sacred offering in error, he must add a fifth to its value and give the sacred offering to the priest.
The priests must not profane the sacred offerings that the Israelites present to the LORD
by allowing the people to eat the sacred offerings and thus to bear the punishment for guilt. For I am the LORD who sanctifies them.”

media.alt: A spotless lamb presented at an altar
media.caption: Every animal offered to the LORD must be without blemish or defect.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,

“Speak to Aaron and his sons and all the Israelites and tell them, ‘Any man of the house of Israel or any foreign resident who presents a gift for a burnt offering to the LORD, whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering,
must offer an {{tamim|unblemished}} male from the cattle, sheep, or goats in order for it to be accepted on your behalf.
You must not present anything with a defect, because it will not be {{ratzon|accepted}} on your behalf.
When a man presents a peace offering to the LORD from the herd or flock to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering, it must be without blemish or defect to be acceptable.

You are not to present to the LORD any animal that is blind, injured, or maimed, or anything with a running sore, a festering rash, or a scab; you must not put any of these on the altar as an offering made by fire to the LORD.
You may present as a freewill offering an ox or sheep that has a deformed or stunted limb, but it is not acceptable in fulfillment of a vow.
You are not to present to the LORD an animal whose testicles are bruised, crushed, torn, or cut; you are not to sacrifice them in your land.
Neither you nor a foreigner shall present food to your God from any such animal. They will not be accepted on your behalf, because they are deformed and flawed.’”

units.label: Practical timing, and God's holy name

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“When an ox, a sheep, or a goat is born, it must remain with its mother for seven days. From the eighth day on, it will be acceptable as an offering made by fire to the LORD.
But you must not slaughter an ox or a sheep on the same day as its young.
When you sacrifice a thank offering to the LORD, offer it so that it may be acceptable on your behalf.
It must be eaten that same day. Do not leave any of it until morning. I am the LORD.
You are to keep My commandments and practice them. I am the LORD.
You must not profane My holy name. I must be acknowledged as holy among the Israelites. I am the LORD who sanctifies you,
who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD.”

qodashim.gloss: Sacred or holy offerings, the food consecrated to God and then given to the priesthood for their sustenance. Treating these carelessly, whether through uncleanness or wrongful sharing, was treated as profaning God's own name.

chalal.gloss: To profane, defile, or treat as common what is holy. Applied throughout this chapter to careless handling of the sacred offerings and, at its close, to God's own holy name, tying priestly conduct directly to God's reputation among the people.

tamim.gloss: Without blemish, whole, or perfect. The required standard for every sacrificial animal, later applied metaphorically to a person's integrity, and ultimately fulfilled in the New Testament's description of Christ as a lamb without blemish or defect.

ratzon.gloss: Acceptance, favor, or that which is pleasing. Repeated throughout this chapter to describe what makes an offering acceptable before God, showing that not every gift, however sincerely offered, automatically pleases Him; the offering itself must meet His standard.

basic.context: This chapter first regulates how priests must be ceremonially clean to eat the sacred offerings, and who within a priestly household may share that food, then turns to require that every animal offered to the LORD, whether for a vow or a freewill offering, be without blemish or defect.
basic.hermeneutics: This is ceremonial and cultic law for Israel's priesthood and sacrificial system, not a dietary or ritual rule for Christians today. Its enduring principle, that God deserves the best, not the leftover or the flawed, and that careless worship dishonors His name, still teaches the church and is fulfilled in Christ's own perfect self-offering.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on qodashim, sacred offerings, which must not be chalal, profaned, by careless priestly conduct, and on tamim, without blemish, the required standard every sacrifice must meet in order to secure ratzon, acceptance, before God.
basic.typology: The requirement that every offering be without blemish anticipates Christ, described as a lamb without blemish or defect, whose precious blood, not silver or gold, secures His people's redemption, 1 Peter 1:18-19.
basic.christ: 1 Peter 1:18-19 says believers were redeemed with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect, exactly the standard this chapter sets for every sacrificial animal. Where Israel had to search their flocks for a flawless animal, God provided, in His own Son, the one perfect and sufficient offering His own law required.
basic.god: God's holiness will not be honored by careless or blemished worship; who may approach His holy things and what may be offered to Him both matter, because His own name and reputation are bound up with how He is worshiped. Yet cleanness is always available through washing and time, and the standard He sets, He Himself ultimately meets in Christ.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 1:18-19 describes believers redeemed by the precious blood of a lamb without blemish. Malachi 1:8 rebukes Israel for later offering God blind, lame, or sick animals, violating this very chapter. Hebrews 10:22 invites believers to draw near with hearts sprinkled clean and bodies washed with pure water. 1 Corinthians 11:27-29 calls believers to examine themselves before approaching the Lord's table.
basic.oneStory: God's insistence on cleanness among His priests and flawlessness in every offering teaches that true worship costs something real and cannot be careless. That standard, impossible for sinful people to meet in themselves, is fulfilled completely when Christ, the spotless Lamb, offers Himself once for all, so that His people can now approach God with clean hearts through Him.

context.literary: This chapter continues the priestly holiness laws begun in chapter 21, moving from the priest's own person to the offerings he handles, and closes the whole run of priestly regulations, chapters 21 through 22, before the appointed feasts begin in chapter 23.
context.historical: As the priesthood's food came directly from what the people consecrated to God, careless handling, whether through uncleanness or wrongful sharing with outsiders, directly threatened both the priesthood's integrity and the people's confidence that their offerings were honored as holy.
context.canonical: This chapter's insistence on unblemished offerings becomes a recurring prophetic complaint, Malachi rebukes Israel for offering blind and lame animals, and it supplies the New Testament's vocabulary for describing Christ Himself as the perfect, spotless sacrifice.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To protect the integrity of Israel's worship at two points, who may eat the sacred food and what may be offered as sacrifice, so that God's holy name is not profaned by carelessness at either point.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is ceremonial law for Israel's priestly households and sacrificial system; it does not bind Christians' food or offering practices today. Its enduring principle, that God deserves the church's best rather than its leftovers, and that worship is never a merely private, careless matter, remains true, fulfilled completely in Christ's perfect self-offering.

debates.question: Is God's demand for an unblemished sacrifice merely about ritual perfectionism?

views.label: Teaching about the cost proper worship requires
views.case: Offering only unblemished, high value animals meant worship cost something real, guarding against the temptation to give God what one would not want oneself, compare Malachi 1:8 and 2 Samuel 24:24.

views.label: Foreshadowing the necessity of a truly sinless substitute
views.case: Others emphasize the requirement's forward looking function: only a flawless sacrifice could adequately picture, and eventually be fulfilled by, a substitute wholly free of the moral defect, sin, that actually separates people from God.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this is not empty ritualism; the standard teaches the costliness proper worship requires and anticipates the sinless substitute these animals could only symbolize.

study.originalLanguages: Qodashim, sacred offerings, must never be chalal, profaned, whether by an unclean priest eating them or by an outsider wrongly sharing in them. The required standard for every animal is tamim, without blemish or whole, since only such an offering secures ratzon, acceptance or favor, before God; sincerity of intent alone was never sufficient under this law.

typology.type: Every offering must be without blemish or defect
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, a lamb without blemish or spot, whose precious blood redeems His people, 1 Peter 1:18-19.

typology.type: Priests excluded from the sacred offerings until clean
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's call to approach God's table only after examining oneself, and the good news that in Christ believers are washed and made fit to draw near, 1 Corinthians 11:27-28, Hebrews 10:22.

study.christ: 1 Peter 1:18-19 declares that believers were redeemed not with perishable things like silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect, precisely the standard this chapter demands of every sacrificial animal. Where Israel had to search their flocks and reject anything flawed, God Himself provided, in His own Son, the one truly perfect offering His own law required but could never itself produce.
study.god: God's holiness will not be honored by careless or blemished worship; both who may approach His holy things and what may be offered to Him matter deeply to Him, because His own reputation among the nations is bound up with how His people worship Him. Yet He is not arbitrary or impossible to please: cleanness is always available through washing and time, and the flawless standard He sets, He Himself ultimately meets on His people's behalf in Christ.

crossReferences.note: Believers are redeemed by the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect, exactly this chapter's required standard.
God later rebukes Israel through Malachi for offering blind, lame, and sick animals, directly violating this chapter's law.
Believers are invited to draw near to God with hearts sprinkled clean and bodies washed with pure water.
Believers are called to examine themselves before approaching the Lord's table, echoing this chapter's concern for approaching holy things rightly.

study.oneStory: God's insistence on cleanness among His priests and flawlessness in every offering teaches Israel, and teaches us, that true worship costs something real and cannot be careless or convenient. That impossible standard is fulfilled completely when Christ, the spotless Lamb, offers Himself once for all, so that His people can now approach a holy God with clean hearts, washed and accepted through Him alone.

quiz.q: What was required of a priest before he could eat the sacred offerings, if he had become ceremonially unclean?

options.text: He had to bathe with water and wait until evening before eating.
options.note: Verses 6-7 specify this exact requirement.

options.text: He was permanently barred from eating the sacred food.
options.note: Verses 6-7 describe a temporary, not permanent, restriction resolved by washing and time.

options.text: No restriction applied to unclean priests.
options.note: Verses 4-6 explicitly restrict an unclean priest from eating the sacred food until clean.

options.text: He had to offer an additional sacrifice before eating.
options.note: The text specifies washing and waiting until evening, not an additional sacrifice.

quiz.q: Who, besides the priest himself, was permitted to eat the sacred offerings within a priestly household?

options.text: Members of his household, including a slave bought with his money or born in his house, and an unmarried or widowed daughter who had returned home.
options.note: Verses 10-13 detail exactly these permitted members.

options.text: Any hired worker or guest visiting the priest's home.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly excludes a hired hand or guest from eating the sacred offerings.

options.text: Any Israelite who happened to be nearby.
options.note: Verse 10 restricts this specifically to the priest's own household, not the general public.

options.text: No one besides the priest himself could ever eat it.
options.note: Verses 11-13 explicitly extend this privilege to certain household members.

quiz.q: What standard did every animal offered to the LORD have to meet, according to verses 19-25?

options.text: It had to be without blemish or defect.
options.note: Verses 19-25 repeat and detail this requirement extensively.

options.text: It had to be the largest animal available.
options.note: The text specifies flawlessness, not size, as the required standard.

options.text: Any animal was acceptable regardless of condition.
options.note: Verses 20 and 22 explicitly reject animals with defects.

options.text: No standard is given for sacrificial animals in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 19-25 give this standard in explicit detail.

quiz.q: How old did an ox, sheep, or goat have to be before it could be offered as a sacrifice, according to verse 27?

options.text: At least eight days old, having remained with its mother for the first seven days.
options.note: Verse 27 states this requirement directly.

options.text: At least one full year old.
options.note: The text specifies eight days as the minimum, not a full year, for this particular requirement.

options.text: No minimum age is specified.
options.note: Verse 27 explicitly gives an eight day minimum.

options.text: It must be newborn, offered on its very first day.
options.note: Verse 27 explicitly requires it remain with its mother for seven days first.

quiz.q: How does 1 Peter 1:18-19 connect to this chapter's standard for sacrificial animals?

options.text: It describes Christ as a lamb without blemish or defect, whose blood redeems believers.
options.note: 1 Peter 1:18-19 uses language that directly echoes this chapter's required standard.

options.text: It rejects the idea that any sacrifice needs to be without blemish.
options.note: 1 Peter 1:19 affirms, rather than rejects, this very standard by applying it to Christ.

options.text: It has no relationship to Old Testament sacrificial standards.
options.note: 1 Peter 1:19 draws directly on this Old Testament vocabulary and standard.

options.text: It applies this standard only to Old Testament animals, never to Christ.
options.note: 1 Peter 1:19 explicitly applies this standard to Christ Himself.

questions: This chapter insists that God's people not treat holy things carelessly. Where in your own life, worship, giving, service, has familiarity bred a kind of casualness that God's holiness deserves better than?
Only an unblemished animal was acceptable, however sincerely offered. Where might you be tempted to offer God your leftovers, time, energy, attention, rather than your best?

Priests had to wait and wash before approaching the sacred offerings again after becoming unclean. What ordinary, patient practices help you return to close fellowship with God after a season of distance or failure?

Christ is the spotless Lamb who met the standard this chapter could only demand, never produce. How does resting in His sufficiency, rather than your own performance, change the way you approach God today?
God cared deeply that His name not be profaned by careless worship among His people. Where do your public words or actions, at church, at work, online, risk misrepresenting God's character to others?

translationNotes.note: Bear the guilt and die reflects the seriousness with which careless priestly handling of sacred things was regarded, treating negligence in worship as a capital, not merely ceremonial, matter.
Add a fifth to its value describes the restitution required for eating a sacred offering by mistake, the same proportion required elsewhere in Leviticus for guilt offerings involving property.

The prohibition on slaughtering a mother and her young on the same day likely reflects a broader biblical concern for mercy even within permitted practices, echoed in Deuteronomy's law against taking a mother bird with her young.
